title: Compression
summary: 9618 AS — lossless vs lossy compression, run-length encoding, and when each technique suits text, images, audio, and video.
learningObjectives: Explain run-length encoding (RLE) with an example.
Describe how JPEG and MP3 use lossy methods.
Evaluate trade-offs between file size and quality.

simpleExplanation.title: Smaller files, same (or nearly same) content
simpleExplanation.summary: Compression reduces storage and transmission cost. Lossless allows perfect reconstruction; lossy discards imperceptible detail for higher ratios.
steps: Lossless: Huffman, RLE, ZIP — text, executables, medical images.
RLE: store count + value for runs (e.g. AAAAB → 4A1B).
Higher compression often means longer encode/decode time.

sections.content: **Compression** (1.3) appears in structured questions on **encoding** and **file formats**. Show a **worked RLE** example and state **why** lossy is acceptable for photos but not for program files.

sections.question: Apply RLE to the string AAAABBBCC.
sections.solution:
Scan left to right:

A×4, B×3, C×2

**Encoded:** 4A3B2C (scheme may vary — state your convention)

steps.caption: Lossless compression — exact reconstruction (ZIP, PNG, FLAC).
Lossy compression discards imperceptible detail (JPEG, MP3).
steps.embedHint: Smaller file but quality loss — irreversible.

steps.caption: Run-length encoding (RLE) for repeated data.
steps.embedHint: AAAABBB → 4A3B — good for flat colour images.

steps.caption: Exam: calculate compressed vs uncompressed file sizes.
steps.embedHint: Apply formula for sound and image data.
